required.title: [Paired Texts] Multiple texts on the same topic representing different perspectives
[Argumentative Texts] Essays, editorials, speeches, and opinion pieces
[Fiction for Critical Analysis] A novel or play that invites critical questioning
required.author: George Orwell, *The Outsiders* by S.E. Hinton, *To Kill a Mockingbird* by Harper Lee, or *A Wrinkle in Time* by Madeleine L'Engle. These are texts where the author's choices

required.title: [Decipherment] Materials on at least one historical decipherment
required.author: James Cross Giblin is excellent for this age.

required.title: [Script Reference] Continued access to script learning materials

suggested.title: [Media Literacy] News articles from multiple outlets covering the same event
[Primary Sources] Historical documents at an appropriate reading level
[For Mentor] How to Read a Book
suggested.author: Mortimer Adler and Charles Van Doren

competence_markers.description: The student can identify the claim, evidence, and reasoning in an argumentative text and evaluate its strength
The student can detect selection bias, framing bias, and language bias in texts and explain their effects
The student can compare two or more texts on the same topic, analyzing differences in perspective, evidence, and framing
The student can synthesize information from multiple sources into a coherent written account that includes their own reasoned position
The student reads fiction critically, analyzing the author's choices and their effects on the reader
The student can narrate at least one historical decipherment and explain what it reveals about writing systems
The student can produce a formal comparison of three or more writing systems along structural dimensions
Written analyses demonstrate clear reasoning, specific textual evidence, and intellectual independence

narration_prompts.prompt: Read this editorial. What is the author's main claim? What evidence do they provide? Is the argument convincing? Why or why not?
narration_prompts.detail: 2. **Here are two accounts of the same event. How do they differ? What does each include that the other leaves out? Why do you think the differences exist?**

narration_prompts.prompt: What bias did you detect in this text? Show me the specific words or choices that reveal the bias. How would the text read if the bias were removed — or reversed?
narration_prompts.detail: 4. **Tell me the story of how [the Rosetta Stone / Linear B] was deciphered. What made it so difficult? What was the breakthrough? What does this tell us about writing systems?**

narration_prompts.prompt: Based on the three sources you read about [topic], what is your understanding? Where do the sources agree? Where do they disagree? What is your own position, and what evidence supports it?
narration_prompts.detail: 6. **Compare the three writing systems you studied. What are the key differences in how they encode language? What are the advantages and disadvantages of each approach?**

cross_connections.description: Evaluating arguments is the core skill of both critical reading and political philosophy. The student who can identify the claim, evidence, and reasoning in an editorial can do the same with a political argument, a legal brief, or a philosophical treatise.

Source analysis applied to historical texts is how historians do their work. The student who reads multiple accounts of the same historical event and evaluates their perspectives is doing real historical analysis.

Analytical writing — the synthesis paper, the argument analysis, the comparative essay — requires the organizational and expressive skills that 3.2.1 develops. The student who reads critically writes better because they understand the choices that authors make.

Bias detection and argument evaluation connect directly to rhetoric — the art of persuasion. The student who can detect bias in a text can also detect rhetorical strategies in a speech, a debate, or a conversation.

Every text reflects a worldview — a set of assumptions about how the world works and what matters. The critical reader is also a worldview analyst, recognizing the philosophical and cultural frameworks that shape what authors write.

The metalinguistic awareness from 3.1.1 supports the language analysis in bias detection — recognizing how word choice and sentence structure shape meaning is a linguistic skill as much as a reading skill.

content.why_this_matters:
You can read closely. You annotate, question, infer, and notice structure. You are no longer a passive reader — you engage with texts as an active thinker. Now the question sharpens: How do you know when to believe what you read?

Everything you read was written by a person with a perspective, a purpose, and limitations. Authors make choices about what to include and what to leave out, what to emphasize and what to minimize, what to state directly and what to imply. Some of these choices are deliberate. Some are unconscious. All of them shape what you receive as a reader. The critical reader is the person who sees these choices — who can identify the author's argument, evaluate the evidence, detect the assumptions, recognize the bias, and make an independent judgment about whether the text deserves their agreement.

This matters because you live in a world saturated with text that is trying to persuade you. News articles frame events to support particular narratives. Advertisements disguise persuasion as information. Political arguments present one side's evidence as the whole truth. Social media posts compress complex issues into confident assertions. The person who reads uncritically accepts all of this at face value. The person who reads critically sees the machinery behind the message.

This packet teaches you to evaluate arguments, analyze sources, compare multiple texts on the same topic, detect bias, and synthesize information from different perspectives into your own informed understanding. These are the skills that make a reader intellectually sovereign — capable of thinking for themselves rather than accepting what they are told.

This packet also completes your Weathering Storms script literacy. You will deepen your understanding of the structural logic behind the world's major script types and study at least one historical decipherment — the detective work by which scholars have cracked the codes of ancient civilizations.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Argument evaluation** — identifying claims, evidence, and reasoning in argumentative texts and assessing their validity
2. **Source analysis** — evaluating who wrote something, why they wrote it, what their perspective and limitations are, and how reliable the text is
3. **Bias detection** — recognizing when an author's perspective systematically distorts the information presented
4. **Synthesis** — integrating information from multiple texts on the same topic into a coherent, independent understanding
5. **Comparative analysis** — reading two or more texts on the same subject and analyzing how they differ in argument, evidence, and perspective
6. **Advanced script literacy** — understanding the structural logic of the major script types and studying historical decipherment as an analytical practice
7. **Intellectual independence** — the habit of forming one's own reasoned judgment rather than accepting or rejecting a text based on who wrote it
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Every Text Has an Author — And Every Author Has a Perspective

This seems obvious, but its implications are not. When you read a text, you are not receiving pure information. You are receiving one person's (or one group's) selected, organized, framed presentation of information. The author chose what to include. They chose what to leave out. They chose how to arrange the information, which details to emphasize, and which words to use.

This does not mean every text is unreliable. It means every text is partial — in both senses of the word. Partial as in incomplete: no text can include everything. And partial as in biased: every author sees the world from a particular position, and that position shapes what they write.

**The questions a critical reader asks:**

- **Who wrote this?** What is their background, expertise, and affiliation? A history of the American Revolution written by a British historian and one written by an American historian may tell very different stories — not because one is lying, but because their positions shape what they notice and emphasize.
- **Why did they write it?** To inform? To persuade? To entertain? To sell something? The purpose shapes the text. A news article and an advertisement about the same product serve very different purposes, and reading them requires different kinds of attention.
- **When and where was it written?** Context matters. A text written during a war reads differently from a text written about that war fifty years later. A text written in the culture it describes reads differently from a text written by an outsider.
- **What is their evidence?** Does the author support claims with specific evidence, or do they assert without support? Is the evidence relevant to the claim? Is it sufficient? Could the same evidence support a different conclusion?
- **What is left out?** This is the hardest and most important question. You can only answer it by reading multiple texts on the same topic and noticing what each one includes that the others do not.

### Part 2: Evaluating Arguments

An argument is not a fight. In analytical reading, an argument is a claim supported by evidence and reasoning. Learning to identify and evaluate arguments is one of the most valuable skills you will ever develop.

**The structure of an argument:**

1. **Claim:** The main point the author is trying to establish. "The school day should start later." "The Roman Empire fell because of overexpansion." "Climate change is primarily caused by human activity."
2. **Evidence:** The facts, data, examples, and observations that support the claim. Statistics, historical records, scientific measurements, expert testimony, specific cases.
3. **Reasoning:** The logic that connects the evidence to the claim. Why does this evidence support this conclusion? What assumptions bridge the gap between the facts and the claim?

**Evaluating an argument:**

- **Is the claim clear?** Can you state exactly what the author is arguing? If the claim is vague or shifting, the argument is weak regardless of the evidence.
- **Is the evidence relevant?** Does the evidence actually support the specific claim being made, or is it about something slightly different?
- **Is the evidence sufficient?** One example does not prove a general claim. A single study does not establish scientific consensus. Is there enough evidence to support the conclusion?
- **Is the reasoning sound?** Does the conclusion follow logically from the evidence? Are there unstated assumptions that, if wrong, would undermine the argument?
- **Are there counterarguments?** Does the author address objections and alternative explanations? An argument that ignores obvious counterarguments is less trustworthy than one that engages with them honestly.

### Part 3: Detecting Bias

Bias is a systematic distortion in how information is selected, presented, or interpreted. Everyone has biases — they are built into human cognition and shaped by culture, experience, and identity. The presence of bias does not make a text worthless. But recognizing bias allows you to adjust for it.

**Types of bias to watch for:**

- **Selection bias:** The author includes evidence that supports their position and omits evidence that contradicts it. This is the most common and most insidious form of bias because the reader sees only what the author chose to show.
- **Framing bias:** The same facts are presented in a way that leads the reader toward a particular conclusion. "The glass is half full" and "the glass is half empty" describe the same reality with different emotional implications. Authors frame constantly.
- **Language bias:** The words an author chooses reveal their stance. "Freedom fighters" versus "terrorists." "Tax relief" versus "tax cuts." "Undocumented immigrants" versus "illegal aliens." The same people or events, described in different words, produce different emotional responses in the reader.
- **Confirmation bias:** Authors (and readers) tend to seek and accept information that confirms what they already believe and to dismiss or ignore information that contradicts it. This is a human universal, and it affects everyone — including you.
- **Source bias:** Who funded the research? Who publishes the newspaper? Who employs the expert? These questions do not automatically invalidate a source, but they are relevant. A study on the health effects of sugar funded by a sugar company deserves more scrutiny than the same study funded by an independent health institute.

**How to detect bias:** Read multiple sources on the same topic. Compare what each includes and omits. Notice the language choices. Ask who benefits from the reader believing this particular version of events. The single source always feels authoritative. The comparison reveals what each source is doing.

### Part 4: Synthesis — Building Your Own Understanding

Synthesis is the act of combining information from multiple sources into a coherent understanding that is your own — not a summary of any single source but an integration of all of them.

**How to synthesize:**

1. **Read multiple texts on the same topic.** Look for what they agree on (this is likely reliable), where they differ (this reveals different perspectives or disputed evidence), and what each includes that the others do not (this reveals selection bias).
2. **Identify the strongest evidence and arguments from each source.** Not all claims are equally supported. Extract the best-supported points from each text.
3. **Note contradictions and unresolved questions.** Where sources disagree, understand why. Is it a factual dispute? A difference in interpretation? A difference in values?
4. **Form your own position.** Based on the evidence and reasoning you have evaluated, what do you think? Your position should be based on evidence, not on which author was most persuasive or which position you held before you started reading.
5. **Acknowledge what you do not know.** Intellectual honesty means recognizing the limits of your understanding. If the evidence is genuinely ambiguous, say so. If you need more information to decide, say so. The critical reader does not pretend to certainty they do not have.

### Part 5: Comparative Analysis — Two Texts, One Topic

One of the most powerful exercises in critical reading is placing two texts side by side and analyzing how they handle the same subject differently.

**What to compare:**

- **What facts does each text include and omit?** The differences reveal each author's priorities and perspectives.
- **How does each text frame the topic?** Is the tone neutral, sympathetic, hostile, urgent, dismissive? How does the framing shape the reader's response?
- **What evidence does each text use?** Are they drawing on the same data and reaching different conclusions, or are they using different data entirely?
- **What assumptions does each text make?** What does each author take for granted that the other does not?
- **What is each text trying to make you think or feel?** This is the deepest question. Every text has an effect on the reader. Identifying that intended effect is the core skill of critical reading.

### Part 6: Advanced Script Literacy — Structure and Decipherment

You have been learning to decode individual scripts. Now step back and understand the structural principles that govern all writing systems.

**The typology of writing systems:**

- **Alphabets** (Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Korean Hangul): Each symbol represents a phoneme (a single sound). Alphabets are efficient — a small set of symbols can represent any word in the language. But they vary in transparency: Finnish and Korean are highly transparent (each letter maps to one sound reliably); English and French are opaque (the same letter can represent different sounds in different words).
- **Abjads** (Arabic, Hebrew): Each symbol represents a consonant. Vowels are either omitted or marked with optional diacritics. Experienced readers of Arabic and Hebrew read without vowel marks, reconstructing the vowels from context and word knowledge. This is a fundamentally different reading process from alphabetic reading.
- **Abugidas** (Devanagari, Thai, Ethiopic Ge'ez): Each symbol represents a consonant-vowel unit. A base consonant form is modified by diacritical marks to indicate different vowels. Devanagari's organization by place and manner of articulation makes it a phonetic chart in script form.
- **Syllabaries** (Japanese hiragana and katakana, Cherokee): Each symbol represents a syllable. Hiragana has about 46 base symbols, each representing a consonant-vowel pair or a lone vowel.
- **Logographic systems** (Chinese characters): Each symbol represents a word or morpheme. Chinese characters are not arbitrary — over 80% are phono-semantic compounds containing a radical that hints at meaning and a component that hints at pronunciation. But the system requires knowledge of thousands of individual characters.

**Decipherment — cracking ancient codes:**

The decipherment of ancient scripts is one of the great intellectual detective stories of human history. Study at least one:

- **The Rosetta Stone and Egyptian hieroglyphs:** In 1799, French soldiers in Egypt found a stone inscribed with the same text in three scripts: Egyptian hieroglyphs, Egyptian demotic, and ancient Greek. Jean-Francois Champollion spent years comparing the scripts and in 1822 proved that hieroglyphs were not purely symbolic — they represented sounds. This single insight unlocked three thousand years of Egyptian written history.
- **Michael Ventris and Linear B:** In 1952, a young English architect named Michael Ventris deciphered Linear B, a script used in Bronze Age Crete and mainland Greece. Working from frequency analysis and inspired guesswork, he proved that Linear B wrote an early form of Greek — centuries earlier than anyone had believed Greek was written. He was 30 years old and had been working on the problem since he was 14.
- **Undeciphered scripts:** The Indus Valley script (approximately 2600–1900 BCE) remains undeciphered. So does Rongorongo, a script from Easter Island. The Voynich Manuscript, a mysterious illustrated text from approximately the 15th century, has never been read. These remind us that the ability to write does not guarantee the ability to be read — and that some human knowledge has been irretrievably lost.

These stories matter because they demonstrate that writing systems are solvable puzzles. They also connect script literacy to the broader intellectual traditions of analysis, pattern recognition, and persistence — the same skills you are developing in every domain.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Argument Analysis [Ongoing — Core Practice]
At least once per week, the student reads an argumentative text (editorial, essay, speech, or opinion piece) and produces a written analysis identifying: the claim, the evidence, the reasoning, any assumptions, any counterarguments addressed or ignored, and the student's own evaluation of the argument's strength. These analyses should be discussed with the mentor.

### Study 2: Paired Text Comparison [Ongoing]
At least twice per month, the student reads two texts on the same topic from different perspectives and produces a written comparison: what each includes and omits, how each frames the topic, what evidence each uses, and where they agree and disagree. The student concludes with their own assessment.

### Study 3: Bias Detection Exercise [Early-to-Mid Packet]
The student reads a news article, editorial, or historical account and identifies specific instances of selection bias, framing bias, and language bias. They rewrite at least one passage to demonstrate how different language choices would change the reader's response. This exercise makes bias detection concrete and practical.

### Study 4: Synthesis Paper [Mid-to-Late Packet]
The student selects a topic of genuine interest, reads at least three sources on that topic, and writes a synthesis paper that integrates the information from all sources into a coherent account. The paper identifies where sources agree and disagree, evaluates the strength of each source's evidence, and presents the student's own informed position. This is the capstone assessment of critical reading.

### Study 5: Novel as Critical Text [Across the Packet]
The student reads a novel critically — not just for plot and character, but for the author's choices and their effects. What perspective is the story told from? Whose voices are heard and whose are absent? What does the author seem to value? How does the novel's structure shape the reader's experience? The student writes a critical response essay.

### Study 6: Decipherment Study [Mid-Packet]
Dedicated study of at least one historical decipherment. The student reads about the process — the clues, the false starts, the breakthroughs — and narrates the story. If studying the Rosetta Stone, the student works with reproductions of the three scripts and traces Champollion's reasoning.

### Study 7: Script Structure Comparison [Late Packet]
The student produces a formal comparison of at least three writing systems across key dimensions: unit of representation, directionality, transparency (how reliably symbols map to sounds), the relationship between form and meaning, and historical origin. This comparison is the Weathering Storms capstone for script literacy.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Argument Evaluation
**Developing well:** The student can dissect an argument into its components (claim, evidence, reasoning) and evaluate each independently. They notice when evidence is insufficient, reasoning is flawed, or counterarguments are ignored. Their evaluations are specific and evidence-based.

**Developing slowly:** The student evaluates arguments based on whether they agree with the conclusion rather than on the quality of the reasoning. They cannot distinguish between a strong argument for a position they dislike and a weak argument for a position they support.

**Diagnostic questions:** Practice with arguments about topics the student has no prior opinion on. This removes the emotional investment and lets the student focus on the reasoning itself. Also practice with deliberately flawed arguments — logical fallacies presented in engaging, persuasive prose — and see whether the student can identify the flaw despite the persuasive packaging.

### 2. Bias Detection
**Developing well:** The student identifies specific instances of bias — pointing to word choices, omitted information, or framing techniques — rather than making vague claims about bias. They can explain how the bias affects the reader's perception. They recognize that bias does not automatically invalidate a source.

**Developing slowly:** The student either does not notice bias or labels everything as biased without specificity. They use "biased" as a dismissal rather than an analytical observation.

**Diagnostic questions:** Give the student the same factual information written in two different frames (same data, different language and emphasis) and ask them to identify how the framing shapes the reader's response. This concrete exercise builds the skill of noticing bias in specific textual choices.

### 3. Synthesis Quality
**Developing well:** The student's synthesis papers integrate information from multiple sources, identify agreements and disagreements, evaluate evidence quality, and present a reasoned position that goes beyond what any single source said. The student acknowledges uncertainty where it exists.

**Developing slowly:** The student's "synthesis" is actually a summary of each source in sequence — "Source 1 said this. Source 2 said this." — without genuine integration. Or the student simply picks the source they like best and ignores the others.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student choosing sources that are different enough to require real synthesis? Three articles that all say the same thing do not challenge the student to integrate. Choose sources that genuinely disagree or emphasize different aspects.

### 4. Critical Reading of Fiction
**Developing well:** The student can analyze a novel's narrative choices — perspective, structure, emphasis, omission — and explain their effects. They can discuss what the author seems to value, whose voices are centered, and how the text would differ if written from a different perspective. They engage with the text as a constructed artifact, not just as a story.

**Developing slowly:** The student reads fiction only at the level of plot and character. They cannot step back to analyze the author's choices. "It was a good book" or "I liked the characters" is the extent of their response.

**Diagnostic questions:** Ask perspective-shifting questions: "What if this story were told from the villain's perspective? What would change?" "What characters are in this book but never get to speak? What might they say if they could?" These questions open the door to critical analysis of fiction.

### 5. Script Literacy and Decipherment Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can explain the structural differences between alphabets, abjads, abugidas, syllabaries, and logographic systems. They can narrate at least one historical decipherment with detail and understanding. They can produce a comparative analysis of writing systems.

**Developing slowly:** The student treats script types as vocabulary to memorize rather than structural categories to understand. They cannot explain why the distinction matters or what it reveals about how reading works.

**Diagnostic questions:** Can the student explain to someone else why Arabic reading is fundamentally different from English reading (not just that the direction is different, but that vowels work differently)? Can they explain why Chinese characters require a different learning strategy than Cyrillic letters? Understanding the structural logic is the goal.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Fostering Cynicism Instead of Criticism
The student who concludes "you can't trust anything" has not learned critical reading — they have learned dismissal. Critical reading is harder than cynicism. It requires the student to take each text seriously, evaluate it fairly, and form a considered judgment. If the student starts dismissing texts without engaging with them, redirect: "Before you decide whether to trust this, tell me what the strongest version of the author's argument is."

### Avoiding Controversial Topics
Critical reading is best learned on texts about things that matter — topics where people genuinely disagree based on different values, different evidence, or different interpretations. Controversial topics are not a problem to avoid; they are the material that makes critical reading real. The key is to present multiple perspectives fairly and to evaluate reasoning rather than policing conclusions.

### Over-Structuring Analysis
If every reading session requires a formal written analysis with claim-evidence-reasoning charts, the student will begin to dread analytical reading. Vary the format. Some analyses are written. Some are oral discussions. Some are quick margin annotations. Some are debates with the mentor. The analytical habit is what matters, not the format.

### Neglecting the Script Work
In a packet focused on critical reading, the script literacy component can feel secondary. Do not let it slip. The script work connects reading to its material foundation — the physical technology of writing. A student who has studied how Champollion deciphered hieroglyphs understands something profound about the relationship between symbol and meaning. That understanding enriches their critical reading of contemporary texts.
